one_line_takeaway: A 72-paper systematic literature review that produces a Taxonomy of RAG Privacy Risks (leakage vs. adversarial manipulation) plus a RAG Privacy Process Diagram and a novel relevance/maturity scoring of mitigations, concluding that proposed RAG privacy mitigations remain largely immature (low code availability, ~24% discuss deployment cost) and skewed toward attack-focused rather than mitigation-focused work.
key_finding: Two main empirical/synthesis findings from the review: (1) Dataset leakage decomposes into two aspects -- leaking data to RAG end users (via PII/sensitive info) and exposing sensitive/confidential information to unauthorized internal users -- and the literature overwhelmingly addresses only the former (via redaction/anonymization), with 'no works' investigating the interconnectedness of the two (e.g., how anonymization complements access control). (2) Across all mitigation categories, the field skews toward attack-focused papers (56% of the 72) versus mitigation-focused papers (36%, with 8% neither), and even where mitigations are proposed, relevance/maturity scores (Table III) are generally low: e.g., Rewriting/Rephrasing for data poisoning scored the highest relevance in the whole table (normalized 1.00) but only 0.37 maturity; roughly 40% of surveyed papers publish reproducible code and only ~24% discuss practical deployment considerations (latency/cost). Yearly growth in this literature went from 3 relevant papers in 2023 to 51 in 2024, per the paper's own count. Evaluation practice (Tables IV-V) is dominated by 'reused' generic QA datasets (Natural Questions, MS-MARCO, HotpotQA, TriviaQA) rather than privacy-purpose-built benchmarks, and metrics span five self-defined groups: retrieval, generation, answer, attack, and 'other' metrics.
core_contribution: This is a Systematic Literature Review (SLR), not an attack or defense paper. Following the Kitchenham et al. SLR methodology, the authors searched Google Scholar, ACM DL, IEEE Xplore (white literature) plus Google Search and YouTube (grey literature) with two search strings ('rag'/'retrieval augmented'/'augmented generation' AND 'private'/'privacy', and AND 'attack'), retrieving 388 results before exclusion, narrowing to 145 after exclusion, and arriving at a final set of 72 relevant papers after a manual relevance check (search performed July 2025). From this corpus they produce two original systematization artifacts: (1) a 'Taxonomy of RAG Privacy Risks' splitting risks into Leakage (five sub-types: dataset, vector database, retrieved chunk, answer, and prompt leakage) and Adversarial Manipulation (eight attack types: jailbreak, backdoor, data poisoning, prompt injection, membership inference, data extraction, prompt extraction, LLM extraction/inversion); and (2) a 'RAG Privacy Process Diagram' mapping where in the RAG pipeline (setup vs. inference/runtime) each risk originates and where mitigations can be applied, distinguishing organizational-internal vs. external actors. They also construct a novel quantitative relevance/maturity scoring scheme (Eq. 1-2) that rates each proposed mitigation category by how many surveyed papers implement it at HIGH/MID/LOW relevance (dedicated section+implementation vs. discussed vs. merely mentioned) and by maturity along reproducibility, cross-domain generalizability, and deployability axes.
threat_model: The paper does not itself assume a single threat model; instead it catalogs threat models found across the 72 surveyed papers. It defines privacy operationally as safeguarding 'private' (personal, not-for-public) and 'confidential' (shared-under-agreement) information from improper disclosure and adversarial threats, treating the two as one umbrella. Adversaries considered in the surveyed literature (not by this paper's own experiments) include: internal actors with unrestricted access to stored documents or the retrieval pipeline; external users issuing crafted or adversarial queries to extract embeddings, memorized training data, or proprietary chunks; and attackers exploiting weak authentication/misconfigured vector-database access controls. The paper explicitly notes adversarial-manipulation attacks (jailbreak, backdoor, poisoning, prompt injection, membership inference, extraction) are covered only briefly since they sit 'on the boundary between security and privacy risks' and readers are directed to the cited works for detail.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: exclusion/filtering and full-text reading were performed solely by the primary researcher, introducing possible researcher bias (mitigated only by weekly team meetings and double-coding of the structured data by two other researchers, not the filtering step itself). The review included non-peer-reviewed preprints, which could affect artifact validity. The taxonomy and Process Diagram model only 'naive' (simple) RAG pipelines; advanced/agentic RAG architectures were explicitly out of scope. The relevance/maturity scoring system is acknowledged as an 'imperfect approximation.' The paper also flags, as open gaps rather than results: no evidence in the surveyed literature of testing mitigations in sequence/combination, no protection proposed anywhere for the mapped text chunks underlying vector embeddings (only the vectors), and very little real-world/live-system testing of privacy risks or mitigations.

quantitative_results: Search yielded 388 results before exclusion, 145 after exclusion, 72 in the final relevant set (Table I)
Google Scholar: 43 (S1) + 72 (S2) after filtering; ACM DL: 9+1; IEEE Xplore: 20+0
48 Google Search results and 6 YouTube videos retained as grey literature
Papers focusing on adversarial attacks: 56% of 72; mitigation-focused: 36%; neither: 8%
~40% of the 72 papers publish reproducible code; ~24% discuss practical deployment considerations (latency/overhead)
Relevant papers per year: 3 in 2023 rising to 51 in 2024 (per the review's own annotation)
Worked example: Differential Privacy for dataset leakage -- relevance = (2x3 HIGH)+(1x0 MID)+(0.5x2 LOW)=7, normalized to 0.5; maturity=(0.2+0.6+0)/3≈0.27
Highest normalized relevance score in Table III: Rewriting/Rephrasing for Data Poisoning at 1.00 (maturity 0.37); Perplexity-based detection for Data Poisoning at 0.85 relevance (0.38 maturity)
